SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper gives a clear constructive mapping from k-tuples of column indices to flat indices (using triangular/tetrahedral numbers) and shows how to use it to produce polynomial expansions directly in CSR, which is logically sound. The combinatorial derivation of the output index and the counting argument for the number of nonzero expansion features are correct in spirit, and the asymptotic bound (essentially O((dD choose k)) ≈ O(d^k D^k) for fixed k) follows from standard combinatorics. However the manuscript contains several typographical/formula formatting issues (e.g. malformed constants in Tk definitions and inconsistent complexity notation) and the empirical comparison omits the time to densify the baseline and lacks hardware/system details or statistical tests, which are minor gaps in experimental support. Overall there are no fundamental technical errors that invalidate the contribution, but presentation and some experimental controls could be improved.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper addresses a practical problem—computing polynomial feature expansions directly on CSR matrices without densification—and presents a simple, concrete algorithm to do so by applying combinatorial index mappings; this is a useful engineering contribution. The core idea is a straightforward application/generalization of well-known combinatorial indexing (triangular/tetrahedral numbers) to sparse feature expansions, so it is more of an incremental/practical advance than a breakthrough. The paper does not situate the contribution strongly against prior work (no related-work discussion in the provided text), so it is not clear how novel this solution is relative to existing sparse-linear-algebra or feature-engineering toolchains.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is organized sensibly (introduction, preliminaries, motivation, mapping, algorithm, complexity, experiments, conclusion) and the main idea is communicated with examples and pseudocode. However the text contains numerous formatting and typographical errors (misplaced numbers in formulas, repeated sentences, malformed constants like '12n(n+1)') and some notation choices (e.g. superscript k used both as label and potentially exponent) that confuse reading; figures referenced in the empirical section are not included in the provided text. With careful proofreading and cleaner presentation of the formulas and experimental details the paper would be much clearer.
